metadata.summary: AI + spatial computing UX: contextual XR adaptation, W3C XAUR gap analysis, three-tier input fallback, on-device latency budget, privacy architecture.

content.title: AI + Spatial Computing
content.summary: AI enables spatial interfaces (AR/VR/MR) to adapt contextually — recognizing environments, predicting gestures, routing voice commands, and powering gaze-based accessibility. This methodology covers AI-driven contextual UI adaptation, W3C XAUR accessibility gap analysis for XR apps, and privacy architecture for biometric/spatial data collection. It gives the agent concrete WHEN triggers, a step-by-step audit checklist, ready-to-paste LLM prompts, an interaction-spec template, CLI commands for measuring on-device inference latency, and a verification procedure that ends with a human-on-hardware sign-off gate.

why.p: Spatial interfaces generate multi-modal sensor data (eye, hand, voice, environment) that static UI patterns cannot handle. AI scene understanding and gesture prediction reduce interaction steps and enable accessibility for motor and visual impairments. Convergence of on-device AI and XR hardware (Vision Pro, Quest 3, HoloLens) creates new UX categories that require purpose-built design patterns. Without an explicit playbook the agent will (a) recommend cloud AI that breaks the 20 ms comfort budget, (b) silently call XAUR gap analyses "compliant", and (c) miss the privacy disclosures required for eye/face/scene data. This file is the playbook.

list.item: Trigger: user input mentions visionOS, Vision Pro, Quest, Quest 3, HoloLens, ARCore, ARKit, RealityKit, OpenXR, Unity Sentis, or "XR/AR/VR/MR accessibility"
Trigger: a spec, design.md, or PRD references gaze, eye tracking, hand tracking, dwell selection, spatial audio, scene anchoring, or passthrough
Trigger: a ticket asks for an XAUR audit, accessibility gap analysis, or accessibility-conformance report on a spatial product
Trigger: code under review includes ARSession, ARFrame, HandTrackingProvider, RealityView, OVRManager, XRHandSubsystem, MediaPipe Hands, or whisper.cpp invocations
Trigger: AI-driven contextual UI adaptation (environment-aware UX) is being added to a spatial application
Trigger: privacy review or DPIA for a product that captures eye, face, hand, room scan, or biometric data
Trigger: ML latency target is being defined for an interactive XR feature (must be <20 ms)
Product targets only 2D web or mobile — spatial patterns add unnecessary complexity
No access to XR hardware for validation — AI-generated spatial UX without device testing is unreliable
Real-time AI inference cannot meet the <20ms threshold — on-device AI is mandatory here
Budget and timeline do not support specialized XR accessibility testing
Feature is non-real-time documentation generation only (e.g. VPAT drafting) — use the generic accessibility-audit methodology instead

how-to.step: Detect a trigger from the when-to-use list and confirm target hardware (Vision Pro, Quest 3, HoloLens 2, or other). Record the device in the working spec; latency budgets and inference paths differ.

Read the existing spec, design.md, or code. Extract: (a) every input modality used, (b) every AI inference call (model, runtime, expected latency), (c) every sensor stream captured (eye, hand, voice, room mesh, face).
Run the audit checklist below end-to-end. Record PASS / FAIL / N/A per item with a one-line rationale. Do not skip items silently.
For each FAIL, write a remediation entry: which input fallback to add, which on-device model to swap in, which consent screen to insert, which latency to re-budget.
Fill in templates/interaction-fallback-spec.md for every interactive surface. Save the populated spec next to the design doc.
Run the XAUR gap-analysis prompt (see prompt block) against the populated spec. Treat output as a structured opinion, never a compliance verdict.
Measure inference latency on the actual target device (see commands block). Reject any user-facing path with p95 ≥ 20 ms.
Run the verification procedure. Block release if any verify step fails. Hand off to a human XR accessibility specialist for on-device testing with users with disabilities.

checklist.check: Confirm target XR hardware is named explicitly (Vision Pro, Quest 3, HoloLens 2, ARCore Android, other) — not "XR" in general
Every interactive surface has a three-tier input fallback documented: gaze → voice → controller/gesture
No interaction depends on a single modality; gaze-only, voice-only, and gesture-only paths each have a fallback
Dwell time for gaze selection is user-configurable (default 600–1000 ms, range 200–2000 ms)
Voice ASR uses on-device model (whisper.cpp tiny/base, Apple Speech, Android SpeechRecognizer offline) for any <200 ms path
All user-facing AI inference runs on-device (Core ML, Sentis, ONNX Runtime, MediaPipe) — cloud AI only for non-real-time tasks
Measured p95 inference latency on the target device is <20 ms for every real-time interaction
Scene-understanding confidence threshold is documented; below threshold the UI surfaces "unknown environment" rather than guessing
Environment context switching (home/office/gym/kitchen) requires explicit opt-in; default is OFF
Eye-tracking calibration flow includes a glasses-wearer path and a nystagmus / poor-calibration fallback
Gesture interactions tested with users who have motor differences, missing fingers, limb differences; sensitivity is configurable
Spatial audio cues have a visual-caption equivalent for d/Deaf and hard-of-hearing users
Vision-impairment path: scene description, object detection, obstacle warning, text-to-speech for in-scene text
Cognitive-load path: step-by-step guidance available, information density configurable, "simplify" toggle present
Privacy: every captured stream (eye, face, hand, room mesh, voice) has a documented retention period
Privacy: GDPR/CCPA-compliant consent screen presented before first use of eye, face, hand, or scene scan
Privacy: data minimization documented — no stream is captured "just in case"
Privacy: an "explain this AI suggestion" affordance is reachable from every AI-driven UI
XAUR gap analysis run against the populated spec; every gap has a confidence label (High/Medium/Low)
Every Low-confidence and Medium-confidence XAUR finding is queued for human XR specialist review
Human-on-hardware sign-off recorded before ship: at least one user with the relevant disability tested on the actual target device
Hallucination guard: every cited XAUR clause cross-checked against https://www.w3.org/TR/xaur/ verbatim
High-stakes contexts (healthcare, finance, navigation): every AI-generated spatial cue validated by a domain expert before deployment

section.name: AI Capabilities in Spatial Computing
section.p: AI capability map for AR/VR/MR interfaces: contextual adaptation, predictive UI, and intelligent object anchoring patterns.

list.item: Scene understanding — auto-adapt UI to environment type; UI appears on walls, tables, floors appropriately
Object recognition — contextual information overlay; point at object, get instant info
Gesture prediction — anticipate user intent; reduce interaction steps
Voice integration — natural language commands; hands-free operation
Personalization — learn user preferences; adapt UI placement, size, frequency
Eye tracking — gaze-based interaction; accessibility for motor disabilities
Hand tracking — precise gesture control; natural interaction without controllers
Spatial audio — 3D sound positioning; navigate by sound; accessibility cue

rule.statement: AI environmental adaptation requires explicit user consent and opt-in — never silent inference from sensor data.
rule.rationale: Always-on cameras and sensors collect biometric and environmental data. Silent context switching (home/office/gym) without disclosure violates user trust and GDPR/CCPA requirements.

list.item: Environment detection: home office (work tools appear), kitchen (recipe UI surfaces), gym (fitness tracking active), meeting room (collaboration tools ready)
Physical constraint adaptation: small room (compact UI), bright lighting (high contrast mode), noisy environment (visual cues emphasized), low light (reduced brightness, larger text)
Time-of-day patterns learned; pre-positions relevant content
Approaching table: document surfaces; looking at product: info card shown
AI confirms before acting — "Look → AI suggests → Confirm" replaces "Click → Navigate → Find → Select"

rule.statement: AI inference latency must be under 20ms for comfortable XR experiences — on-device processing is mandatory; cloud AI introduces disqualifying latency.
rule.rationale: Latency above 20ms causes nausea and discomfort in spatial environments. Cloud round-trips cannot meet this threshold; all user-facing AI in XR must run on-device.

list.item: Apple Vision Pro — on-device AI via Core ML
Meta Quest — Mixed Reality Utility Kit; AI avatars and translation
Microsoft HoloLens — Azure Spatial Anchors; cloud AI for non-real-time features only
Google ARCore — scene understanding, ML Kit integration
Unity Sentis — on-device model inference in Unity XR apps

section.name: XR Accessibility with AI
section.p: XAUR coverage by disability type, three-tier input fallback hierarchy, and privacy architecture requirements for spatial computing.

list.item: Vision: AI describes spatial environment for blind users; object detection with audio feedback; obstacle detection; text recognition and reading aloud; scene summarization
Motor: gaze-based selection (eye tracking); voice commands for all interactions; AI gesture simplification (reduce precision needed); dwell-time activation; head-tracking alternatives
Cognitive: AI simplifies complex spatial interfaces; step-by-step guidance with visual arrows; reduce information density; context-aware help; predictive text for spatial keyboards
Hearing: visual captions for spatial audio; haptic feedback for sound cues; visual indicators for directional sound; AI lip-reading for avatars

rule.statement: Design all spatial interactions with a three-tier fallback hierarchy — gaze → voice → controller/gesture. Never assume a single input modality.
rule.rationale: Motor, vision, or hearing impairments may prevent use of any single modality. Three-tier fallback ensures the interaction is accessible even when two of the three modalities are unavailable.

rule.statement: Run W3C XAUR gap analysis early in design, not after implementation — spatial accessibility retrofits are expensive.
rule.rationale: Spatial interfaces are harder to retrofit for accessibility than web interfaces. Core input methods and scene understanding architecture decisions made early determine what accessibility is achievable.

rule.statement: XAUR is not machine-checkable — treat agent gap analysis as a structured opinion, not a compliance verdict. Human XR accessibility expert must validate on actual hardware.
rule.rationale: No automated tool covers XR accessibility criteria as of 2026. Agent analysis is input for human review, not a substitute.

list.item: Local processing where possible — biometric and spatial data should not leave device for real-time features
Clear privacy controls with explicit opt-in for AI features
Data minimization — collect only what is needed for the specific interaction
Transparent AI decision-making — users must be able to understand and override AI behavior
GDPR/CCPA disclosure required for eye tracking, face tracking, hand tracking, and scene scanning

section.name: Spatial Computing AI Anti-Patterns
section.p: Latency constraints, gesture model bias, gaze calibration failures, on-device compute limits, and agent hallucination risks in XR development.

antipattern.description: Using cloud AI for real-time spatial interactions requiring <20ms latency.
antipattern.reason: Cloud round-trips cannot meet the 20ms comfort threshold for XR. All user-facing AI in spatial environments must run on-device. Cloud AI is only acceptable for non-real-time features (VPAT drafting, documentation generation).

antipattern.description: Designing gesture interactions without testing with users who have motor differences.
antipattern.reason: Gesture prediction models are trained on majority-population hand shapes. Users with motor differences, missing fingers, or limb differences experience significantly higher error rates. This is not solvable by AI alone — it requires inclusive hardware design and configurable gesture sensitivity.

antipattern.description: Deploying gaze-based selection without testing on users who wear glasses or have nystagmus.
antipattern.reason: Eye tracking calibration fails for common conditions. AI cannot compensate for poor calibration. Gaze-based interactions must always have a non-gaze fallback.

antipattern.description: Agent XAUR gap analysis presented as a compliance verdict without human expert validation on actual hardware.
antipattern.reason: XAUR is not machine-checkable. Agent outputs are a structured starting point. Spatial accessibility assessment requires testing with people with disabilities on actual XR hardware — no agent can substitute.

antipattern.description: AI companion or avatar features deployed in high-stakes contexts (healthcare navigation, financial guidance) without expert validation of every AI-generated cue.
antipattern.reason: Hallucination risk is high for XR accessibility — this is a thin domain in LLM training data. XAUR references generated by agents must be cross-checked against the actual W3C spec.

antipattern.description: Recommending on-device AI capabilities without accounting for the constrained compute envelope of target hardware.
antipattern.reason: Quest 3 and Vision Pro have very different compute budgets. Agent recommendations must account for the specific device's thermal, battery, and inference constraints — cloud-quality AI is not available on most current XR hardware.

list.item: Design all spatial interactions with a three-tier fallback: gaze → voice → controller/gesture — never assume a single input modality
Validate AI latency on actual target hardware, not simulation — Quest 3 and Vision Pro have very different compute envelopes
Test gaze interaction with users who wear glasses and those with nystagmus — AI calibration assumptions break here
AI environmental adaptation (home/office/gym context switching) requires explicit user consent and opt-in, not silent inference
For healthcare or navigation use cases, require a human expert to validate every AI-generated spatial cue before deployment
Document privacy data flows explicitly in the spec — biometric and spatial data collection requires GDPR/CCPA disclosure even in XR apps
Run XAUR gap analysis early in design, not after implementation — spatial accessibility retrofits are expensive
XAUR is not machine-checkable; agent gap analysis is a structured opinion, not a compliance verdict — human XR accessibility expert must validate
AI scene understanding confidence drops in novel environments the training set did not cover — agent must flag low-confidence recommendations rather than assert them
On-device model inference limits mean the agent cannot assume cloud-quality AI in the production XR app; recommendations must account for constrained compute
Gesture prediction models biased toward neurotypical hand shapes — agent must explicitly flag that gesture interactions require testing with users with motor differences
Human-in-loop checkpoint: before any XR accessibility feature ships, a user with the relevant disability must test it on the target device
Agent hallucination risk is high for XR accessibility — this is a thin domain in LLM training data; cross-check all specific XAUR references against the actual W3C spec
ARCore CLI tools — Test AR scene understanding on Android devices | developer.android.com/ar
Xcode Accessibility Inspector — Inspect accessibility properties of visionOS apps | Bundled with Xcode
whisper.cpp — On-device speech-to-text (C++ port, low-latency) | github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp
mediapipe — On-device hand/pose tracking for gesture prediction | pip install mediapipe / mediapipe.dev

section.name: Services and Apps
section.p: XR platforms, AI services, and their agent compatibility.

list.item: Apple Vision Pro SDK (visionOS) (Platform SDK, Partial) — AI integration via Core ML; agent can generate boilerplate
Meta Quest SDK (Platform SDK, Partial) — Mixed Reality Utility Kit; agent can scaffold patterns
Microsoft Azure Spatial Anchors (SaaS, Yes) — Persistent object anchoring with cloud AI; API-driven
Google ARCore (Platform SDK, Yes) — Scene understanding, object recognition; ML Kit integration
Azure AI Vision (SaaS, Yes) — Scene description for blind users; spatial audio cue generation
OpenAI Whisper (cloud) (SaaS, Yes) — High-accuracy voice commands; requires network (latency trade-off)
Unity Sentis (OSS/SDK, Yes) — On-device model inference in Unity XR apps

commands.command: git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/whisper.cpp && cd whisper.cpp && make -j && bash ./models/download-ggml-model.sh tiny.en

python3 -c "import time, mediapipe as mp, cv2, numpy as np; h=mp.solutions.hands.Hands(); img=np.zeros((480,640,3),np.uint8); ts=[] for _ in range(100): t=time.perf_counter(); h.process(cv2.cvtColor(img,cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)); ts.append((time.perf_counter()-t)*1000) ts.sort(); print(f'p50={ts[50]:.1f}ms p95={ts[94]:.1f}ms p99={ts[98]:.1f}ms')"
xcrun xctrace record --template "Time Profiler" --device "Apple Vision Pro" --launch -- com.example.YourXRApp --output /tmp/xr-trace.trace
adb logcat -s OVRPlugin:I MRUK:I XR-Perf:I | tee /tmp/quest-perf.log
# In Unity: Window → Analysis → Profiler; deploy with Build Settings → Universal Windows Platform → Run on device → check XR latency markers

template.purpose: Spatial interaction pattern spec: primary/secondary/tertiary input + privacy data fields
Sonnet prompt: XR design spec → structured XAUR gap analysis with recommendations

verify.step: Run python3 scripts/validate-methodology-xml.py skills/faion/knowledge/geek/ux/accessibility-specialist/ai-spatial-computing/methodology.xml — must print PASS.
Confirm every checklist item has a recorded PASS / FAIL / N/A with a one-line rationale stored in the working spec or PR description.
Confirm templates/interaction-fallback-spec.md is filled in for every interactive surface and committed alongside the design doc.
Run the XAUR gap-analysis prompt against the populated spec; confirm output is valid JSON matching the schema in the prompt block.
Measure on-device inference latency with the relevant commands entry on the actual target device; reject if any user-facing path has p95 ≥ 20 ms.
Cross-check every cited XAUR clause against https://www.w3.org/TR/xaur/ verbatim. If a clause cannot be quoted, mark confidence Low and queue for human review.
Privacy: confirm consent screens for eye/face/hand/scene/voice exist, retention is documented, and an "explain this AI suggestion" affordance is reachable from each AI-driven UI.
Human-on-hardware sign-off: at least one user with the relevant disability tested the feature on the actual target device. Record name, device, date, outcome. Block ship without this entry.
For high-stakes contexts (healthcare, finance, navigation), confirm a domain expert has validated every AI-generated spatial cue. Block ship without this entry.
